title: Hessian Eigenspectra of More Realistic Nonlinear Models [PDF]

## **Hessian Eigenspectra of More Realistic Nonlinear Models**

**Zhenyu Liao** _[∗†]_ School of Electronic Information and Communications Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China `zhenyu_liao@hust.edu.cn`
**Michael W. Mahoney** ICSI and Department of Statistics University of California, Berkeley, USA `mmahoney@stat.berkeley.edu`

## **Abstract**

Given an optimization problem, the Hessian matrix and its eigenspectrum can be used in many ways, ranging from designing more efficient second-order algorithms to performing model analysis and regression diagnostics. When nonlinear models and non-convex problems are considered, strong simplifying assumptions are often made to make Hessian spectral analysis more tractable. This leads to the question of how relevant the conclusions of such analyses are for realistic nonlinear models. In this paper, we exploit tools from random matrix theory to make a _precise_ characterization of the Hessian eigenspectra for a broad family of nonlinear models that extends the classical generalized linear models, without relying on strong simplifying assumptions used previously. We show that, depending on the data properties, the nonlinear response model, and the loss function, the Hessian can have _qualitatively_ different spectral behaviors: of bounded or unbounded support, with single- or multi-bulk, and with isolated eigenvalues on the left- or right-hand side of the main eigenvalue bulk. By focusing on such a simple but nontrivial model, our analysis takes a step forward to unveil the theoretical origin of many visually striking features observed in more realistic machine learning models.

## **1 Introduction**

The Hessian is ubiquitous in applied mathematics, statistics, and machine learning (ML). Given a (loss) function _L_ ( **w** ) of some parameters **w** _∈_ R _[p]_ , the Hessian **H** ( **w** ) _∈_ R _[p][×][p]_ is defined as the second derivative of the loss function with respect to the model parameter, i.e., **H** ( **w** ) = _∂L_ ( **w** ) _/_ ( _∂_ **w** _∂_ **w**[T] ). When a ML model is being trained, it is common to parameterize that model by **w** , and then train that model by minimizing some (smooth) loss function _L_ ( **w** ), with the associated Hessian **H** ( **w** ), e.g., by backpropagating the error to improve **w** [26]. Alternatively, once a ML model is trained, the Hessian (and the related Fisher information matrix [66, 68]) can be examined to identify outliers, perform diagnostics, and/or engage in other sorts of model validation [31, 79, 62].

For convex problems, the Hessian **H** ( **w** ) provides detailed information on how to adjust the gradient to achieve improved convergence, e.g., in Newton-like methods. For non-convex problems, the properties of the local loss “landscape” around a given point **w** in the parameter space is of central

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

significance [18, 36, 13, 39, 77, 78, 79]. In this case, most obviously, the signs of the smallest and largest Hessian eigenvalue can be used to test whether a given **w** is a local maximum, local minimum, or a saddle point. More subtly, the Hessian eigenvalue distribution characterizes the local curvature of the loss function and provides direct access to, for instance, the fraction of negative Hessian eigenvalues that determines the number of (local) descent directions, a quantity that is directly connected to the rates of convergence of various optimization algorithms [33].

For theoretical analysis of neural network (NN) models, Hessian eigenspectra are often assumed to follow well-known random matrix distributions such the Marcenko–Pastur law [˘ 45] or the Wigner’s semicircle law [70]. This enables one to use Random Matrix Theory (RMT), but it involves (for NNs, at least) making relatively strong simplifying assumptions (e.g., the Hessian can be decomposed as the sum of the two freely independent matrices, the residual error, data feature, and weights are all composed of i.i.d. zero mean normal random variables) [57, 58, 15]. A somewhat more realistic setup involves using a so-called _spiked model_ (or a spiked covariance model) [3, 5, 41]. In this case, the matrix follows a _signal-plus-noise_ model and consists of _full rank_ random noise matrix and _low rank_ statistical information structure.[3] The “signal” eigenvalues are generally larger than the noisy “bulk” eigenvalues; and the maximum eigenvalues, when isolated from the bulk, are referred to as the “spikes.” A substantial theory-practice gap exists, however. In both toy examples [27] and state-of-the-art NN models [77, 78, 79, 80, 64, 20], the strong simplifying assumptions are far from satisfactory. (A similar theory-practice gap has been observed for other NN matrices to which RMT has been applied, perhaps most notably weight matrices [47, 48, 46].) A more precise understanding of the Hessian eigenspectra (and its dependence on the input data structure, the underlying response model and model parameters, as well as the loss function) for more realistic models is needed.

## **1.1 Our approach**

In this article, we address these issues, in a setting that is simple enough to be analytically tractable but complex enough to shed light on realistic large-scale models. We consider a family of generalized generalized linear models (G-GLMs) that extends the popular generalized linear model (GLM) [19, 31]; and we show that, even for such simple models, the key simplifying assumptions used in previous theoretical analyses of Hessian can be very inexact. In particular, apart from a few special cases (including linear least squares and logistic regression with homogeneous features), most Hessians of G-GLMs are _not_ close to the Marcenko–Pastur and/or the semicircle law.˘ Instead, the corresponding Hessian depends on the input feature structure, the underlying response model, and the loss function, in a more involved fashion that can be precisely characterized by the proposed analysis.

The G-GLM describes a generalized linear relation between the input feature **x** _i ∈_ R _[p]_ and the corresponding response _yi_ , in the sense that there exists some parameters **w** _∗ ∈_ R _[p]_ such that for given **w** _∗_[T] **[x]** _[i]_[, the response] _[ y][i]_[is independently drawn from]

## **1.2 Our main contributions**

The main contribution of this work is the _exact_ characterization of Hessian eigenspectra for the family of G-GLMs, in the high-dimensional regime where the feature dimension _p_ and the sample size _n_ are both large and comparable. Precisely, we establish:

1. the limiting eigenvalue distribution of the Hessian matrix (Theorem 1); and

2. the behavior of (possible) isolated eigenvalue-eigenvector pairs (Theorem 2 and 3),

as a function of the dimension ratio _c_ = lim _p/n_ , feature statistics, the loss function _ℓ_ in (3), and the underlying response model in (1). Our results are based on a technical result of independent interest:

We also demonstrate our results empirically by showing that:

4. for a given response model (1), the Hessian eigenvalue distribution depends on the choice of loss function and the data/feature statistics in an intrinsic manner, e.g., bounded versus unbounded support and single- versus multi-bulk in Fig 2; and

5. there may exist two _qualitatively_ different spikes—one due to data _signal_ _**µ**_ and the other due to **w** _∗_ or **w** and thus the _underlying model_ —which may appear on different sides of the main bulk, and their associated phase transition behaviors are characterized (Fig 4 versus 5).

To have a more clear picture of our contribution, we compare, in Fig 1a and 1b, the Hessian eigenvalues for the logistic model (2) with the logistic loss _ℓ_ ( _y, h_ ) = ln(1 + _e[−][yh]_ ) (which is the loss of choice within the maximum likelihood framework), for different choices of **w** in the parameter space. A nontrivial interplay between the response model, feature statistics and the parameter **w** is reflected by the range of the Hessian eigenvalue support and an additional right-hand spike in Fig 1b, as confirmed by our theory. For phase retrieval model _y_ = ( **w** _∗_[T] **[x]**[)][2][with square loss] _[ ℓ]_[(] _[y, h]_[) = (] _[y][ −][h]_[2][)][2] _[/]_[4][, the] non-convex nature of the problem is reflected by a (relatively large) fraction of negative Hessian eigenvalues in Fig 1c. We also note that the top eigenvector (that corresponds to the largest eigenvalue) contains structural information of the underlying model, in the sense that it is positively correlated with **w** _∗_ , as predicted by our theory. This is indeed connected to the Hessian-based initialization scheme widely used in non-convex problems [8, 37, 34, 40, 2, 51].
We conclude by emphasizing that, by focusing on the simple yet fundamental G-GLM, we obtain results that improve upon and are different than previous efforts in the following aspects:

- (i) We provide _precise_ asymptotic characterizations of the Hessian eigenspectra that go beyond, e.g., [7], where only Hessian lower bounds are given in the case of logistic model with logistic loss: our methodology and theoretical results hold much more generally for the family of G-GLM with arbitrary loss. As illustrating examples, we discuss linear least squares in Sec 3.1, logistic model with different choices of loss function in Fig 2, phase retrieval model in Figure 1c, and more in [43, Sec 4].
- (ii) We extend the results in [57, 53, 44, 50] to G-GLMs by considering _generic_ data statistics and loss function, whereas in previous efforts only much more homogeneous models are

## **1.3 Related work**

Here, we provide a brief review of related previous efforts, see more discussions in [43].

**Random matrix theory.** Random matrices of the type (4) are related to the _separable covariance_ 1 1 _model_ [81, 17] in the RMT literature, which is of the form **C** 2 **ZDZ**[T] **C** 2 , for **Z** random and **C** _,_ **D** deterministic or random but _independent_ of **Z** . Our results generalize this, by allowing **D** to _depend_ on **Z** , in a possibly nonlinear fashion, per (4). This is of direct interest for the Hessian of G-GLMs.

**Hessian eigenspectra.** The eigenspectra of Hessian matrices arising in ML models (in particular, for NNs) have attracted considerable interest recently [60, 61, 11, 22, 72, 24, 32, 20, 64, 79, 80, 25]. However, these investigations are often built upon somewhat unrealistic simplifying assumptions and reduce to the “mixed” behavior of Marcenko–Pastur and semicircle law [˘ 57, 15]. In contrast, here we focus on the more tractable example of G-GLM and provide _precise_ results on the Hessian eigenspectra for structural feature on arbitrary loss. Efforts have also been devoted to efficiently computing the eigenspectra of Hessian matrices, which, despite not enabling analytic results, allows for the computation or approximation of Hessian spectra for large-scale ML models such as DNNs, without any assumption on the data or underlying (response) model [30, 1, 79, 74, 25].

**Spectral initialization in non-convex problems.** A popular initialization scheme (of gradient-based methods) for non-convex problems is the _spectral initialization_ , where the top eigenvectors of some Hessian-type matrices are used as gradient descent initialization [8, 37, 34, 40, 2, 51]. In [44], which was generalized in [50], the authors evaluated the eigenspectrum asymptotics of _n_[1] � _ni_ =1 _[f]_[(] _[y][i]_[)] **[x]** _[i]_ **[x]** _i_[T][,] for **x** _i ∼N_ ( **0** _,_ **I** _p_ ). Their technical approach is, however, limited to the case of very homogeneous features with **x** _i ∼N_ ( **0** _,_ **I** _p_ ). Here we generalize the analysis in [44, 50] to the Hessian of G-GLM, by developing a systematic approach to account for both feature structures and loss functions.

**Scalable second-order methods.** Second-order methods are among the most powerful optimization methods that have been designed, and there have been several attempts to use their many advantages for machine learning applications [75, 69, 59], particularly for training NNs [79, 80, 64, 20, 73]. We

4

expect that our precise characterization of the Hessian sheds new light on the understanding and improved design of (e.g., computationally) more efficient second-order methods.

**Spectra of realistic NN weight matrices.** Recent work demonstrated that the eigenvalue distribution of weight matrices of state-of-the-art DNN models in computer vision and natural language applications exhibit heavy-tailed (i.e., _not_ Marcenko–Pastur) behavior, and that this can be used to define˘ metrics to predict trends in the quality of state-of-the-art NNs without access to training or testing data [47, 48, 46]. It would be of interest to extend our approach to weight matrix analysis.

## **2 Main results**

In the section, we present our main results: on the limiting Hessian eigenspectrum (in Sec 2.1); and on the behavior of the (possible) isolated eigenvalue-eigenvector(s) (in Sec 2.2). These two main results depend on a technical deterministic equivalent result for the Hessian resolvent (in Sec 2.3), which is of independent interest. We position ourselves in the following high-dimensional regime.

**Assumption 1** (High-dimensional asymptotics) **.** _As n, p →∞ with p/n → c ∈_ (0 _, ∞_ ) _, we have i.i.d._ max _{∥_ **w** _∥, ∥_ **w** _∗∥}_ = _O_ (1) _and_ **x** _i ∼N_ ( _**µ** ,_ **C** ) _with_ max _{∥_ _**µ** ∥, ∥_ **C** _∥}_ = _O_ (1) _._

Clearly, depending on the measures _ν_ (of _g_ , which depends on feature statistics, loss and underlying model) and _ν_ ˜ (of spectrum of feature covariance **C** ), the Hessian spectrum can have very different forms. Here we compare the empirical Hessian eigenvalues with their limiting behaviors per Theorem 1 for different feature covariance structures[7] . In particular, one may observe a single main bulk with more “compact” Hessian spectrum as in Fig 2c or multiple bulks (two in the case of Fig 2d) with Hessian eigenvalues more “spread-out”, depending on the feature covariance structure _ν_ ˜. In the form of (7), the condition for the existence of multi-bulk eigenspectrum has been thoroughly discussed in [17, Sec 3.2–3.4] and can be numerically evaluated with ease.

As a side remark, the “multi-bulk” behavior similar to Fig 2d has been empirically observed in Hessians of NNs in [42, 53] and is believed to be due to the classification structure within data (i.e., the data vectors are drawn from a mixture of distributions). Here, we provide an alternative explanation via feature covariance structure that holds beyond the classification setting.

## **2.2 Isolated eigenvalues and eigenvectors**

As discussed in Remark 1, under Assumption 1, the Hessian has bounded (limiting) eigen-support if and only if _ν_ , the law of _g_ , has bounded support. Under this condition (or, after the application of some function _f_ so that _f_ ( _g_ ) is bounded), we can then talk about the (possible) isolated Hessian eigenvalues, as in the following result, the proof of which is given in [43, Sec A.3].

Theorem 2 provides an asymptotic characterization of the possible isolated Hessian eigenvalues by computing the determinant of the much smaller (three-by-three) deterministic matrix **G** closely related to the key quantity _δ_ ( _z_ ) defined in Theorem 1. Note that, Theorem 2 does not provide, at least explicitly, the _phase transition_ condition under which these spikes become “isolated” from the main bulk. As we shall see in more details in Sec 3.2, two types of quantitatively different phase transitions can be characterized, due to the data “signal” _**µ**_ and the underlying model, respectively.

We can also analyze the associated isolated eigenvectors. The situation here is more subtle (than the spike due to data signal discussed in Sec 3.2.1): as the model parameter **w** changes (e.g., as the “energy” _∥_ **w** _∥_ grows), both the Hessian (limiting) eigenvalue distribution and the possible spike location are impacted. Fig 5 illustrates the behavior of the spike due to **w** in the setting of Corollary 2. Note first that, different from the case of spike due to data signal _**µ**_ , the spike in Fig 5-(left) appears on the _left-hand side_ of the main bulk: this particularly means that the Hessian may admit an eigenvalue that is _significantly smaller_ than all the other eigenvalues.[8] Also, note from Fig 5-(middle) that, different from the spike due to _**µ**_ , the spike due to **w** exhibits here a “non-monotonic” behavior in the sense that, it is absent for small values of _∥_ **w** _∥_ (as for small _∥_ _**µ** ∥_ in Fig 4-middle) and becomes “isolated” as _∥_ **w** _∥_ increases, but then again “merges into” the main bulk as _∥_ **w** _∥_ continues to increase, resulting an eigengap that falls back to zero.

It is worthy mentioning that, while, technically speaking, the proposed analysis is not capable of characterizing the behavior as _∥_ **w** _∥→∞_ under Assumption 1, empirical results suggest that for extremely large _∥_ **w** _∥_ , the eigengap tends to “vanish”, the associated dominant eigenvector can still be used to recover **w** almost perfectly, see [43, Fig 8] as an example.

## **4 Conclusion**

In this article, we provided a precise asymptotic characterization of Hessian eigenspectra (including the limiting eigenvalue distribution and the behavior of possible isolated eigenvalue-eigenvector pairs) for the family of G-GLMs via a random matrix analysis. By assuming generic Gaussian input features, our results show that the Hessian can have a “heavy-tailed” or multi-bulk spectral behavior, and with isolated eigenvalues of the left- or right-hand side of the main bulk, depending on the underlying GLM and the choice of loss function.

Extending our approach to more involved ML models such as multi-layer neural networks is challenging, e.g., one will need to first study the layer-wise Hessian and evaluate how the objective of interest (e.g., the eigenvalue distribution) of the full Hessian depends on each of its (layer-wise) blocks. Simplification can be made by specifying the choice of loss function and/or using different approximations (such as Gauss-Newton-type approximation [63] or working in the infinitely-wide but less-realistic NTK regime [32]), so as to facilitate analysis. We leave this for future work.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper addresses a timely and well-motivated problem: understanding Hessian eigenspectra in nonlinear models beyond the restrictive assumptions (Marčenko–Pastur, Wigner semicircle, free independence) commonly used in prior random matrix theory (RMT) analyses of neural networks. The introduction is clearly written and effectively situates the work within both the theoretical RMT literature and the empirical observations made on realistic deep learning models, and the proposed "generalized generalized linear model" (G-GLM) framework is a sensible intermediate abstraction that is analytically tractable while accommodating phase retrieval, nonlinear factor models, and single-layer networks. That said, several concerns stand out. First, although the authors emphasize a "theory-practice gap," the G-GLM is still a substantial simplification relative to deep networks, and the paper (at least in this excerpt) does not make explicit what qualitative deep-learning phenomena the analysis demonstrably explains versus merely gestures toward — the claim that the work "unveils the theoretical origin of many visually striking features observed in more realistic ML models" is strong and should be calibrated or backed by concrete empirical comparisons. Second, the novelty relative to existing spiked-model and RMT analyses of GLMs (e.g., work by El Karoui, Donoho-Montanari, Mai-Liao) should be delineated more sharply; the current framing risks understating prior contributions. Third, the writing occasionally conflates distinct technical objects (e.g., Hessian vs. Fisher information vs. loss landscape curvature) in ways that could confuse readers, and the informative but dense footnote deferring proofs to an extended version raises reproducibility/verification concerns for a conference submission. Finally, the clarity would benefit from an explicit, upfront summary of the key qualitative phenomena (single- vs. multi-bulk, left- vs. right-side spikes) tied to specific combinations of data/model/loss, ideally with a guiding figure, so that readers can immediately grasp the scope and novelty of the characterization before entering the technical development.

comments.title: Hessian definition uses denominator notation that obscures a transpose
comments.explanation: The standard definition of the Hessian is H_{ij} = ∂²L/(∂w_i ∂w_j), yielding a symmetric p×p matrix. The notation ∂L(w)/(∂w ∂w^T) is non-standard — it reads as a first derivative divided by a product of vectors. The intended meaning is the second derivative ∂²L(w)/(∂w ∂w^T), i.e., the numerator should be ∂²L. This appears to be a typo or OCR artifact dropping the superscript 2. Since the paper relies on this object throughout, a reader encountering only this definition could be momentarily confused, though context makes the intent clear. Minor because any reader in the field immediately recognizes the Hessian.

comments.title: Phase retrieval Hessian eigenvalue: sign/form of g appears inconsistent with square loss
comments.quote: the phase retrieval model with _y_ = ( **w** _∗_[T] **[x]**[)][2][ and square loss] _[ ℓ]_[(] _[y, h]_[) =] ( _y − h_[2] )[2] _/_ 4, for which we have _g_ = 3( **w**[T] **x** )[2] _−_ ( **w** _∗_[T] **[x]**[)][2]
comments.explanation: The paper defines g ≡ ∂²ℓ(y,h)/∂h². For ℓ(y,h) = (y-h²)²/4, we have ∂ℓ/∂h = -h(y-h²) = h³ - yh, and ∂²ℓ/∂h² = 3h² - y. With y = (w*ᵀx)² and h = wᵀx, we get g = 3(wᵀx)² - (w*ᵀx)², which matches. Then with w = w*, g = 2(wᵀx)². The paper then claims 'with say w* = w, g follows a chi-square distribution with one degree of freedom.' A chi-square with one df would arise from (wᵀx)² when wᵀx ~ N(0,1); so g = 2(wᵀx)² is 2 times a chi-square with one df, which is a scaled chi-square. This is a minor imprecision but the main claim (unbounded support) is correct.

comments.quote: consider the logistic model (2) with _ℓ_ ( _y, h_ ) = exp( _−yh_ ), we have that _g_ = exp( _−yh_ )
comments.explanation: The paper defines g ≡ ∂²ℓ(y,h)/∂h² (the second derivative of the loss with respect to h). For ℓ(y,h) = exp(-yh), we have ∂ℓ/∂h = -y·exp(-yh) and ∂²ℓ/∂h² = y²·exp(-yh). For the logistic model where y ∈ {-1,+1}, y² = 1, so g = exp(-yh). So the final expression is correct for binary y ∈ {±1}, but the text writes 'g = exp(-yh)' as if directly, omitting the y² factor. This is fine under the binary labeling convention but could confuse readers who don't immediately recognize y² = 1. The conclusion (log-normal, unbounded support) is correct since yh ~ N with y ∈ {±1} gives -yh still normal. This is a minor issue of implicit assumption.

comments.title: Claim that g ≤ 1/4 for logistic loss stated without the y² factor context
comments.quote: with logistic loss _ℓ_ ( _y, h_ ) = ln(1 + _e[−][yh]_ ), one has _g ≤_ 1 _/_ 4
comments.explanation: For ℓ(y,h) = ln(1+e^{-yh}), the second derivative with respect to h is y²·e^{-yh}/(1+e^{-yh})². For binary y ∈ {±1}, y²=1 and the expression is bounded by 1/4 (maximum of σ(u)(1-σ(u))). This is correct under the standard binary classification convention. Not really an issue, just noting the implicit convention is consistent with the earlier exponential loss example.

comments.title: Square loss does not give g = 1 in the G-GLM Hessian
comments.quote: say _g_ = 1: this happens, e.g., when the square loss _ℓ_ ( _y, h_ ) = ( _y − h_ )[2] _/_ 2 is employed.
comments.explanation: The authors define g ≡ ∂²ℓ/∂h². For the square loss ℓ(y,h) = (y−h)²/2, we have ∂ℓ/∂h = −(y−h) = h−y, and ∂²ℓ/∂h² = 1. So g = 1 indeed. This checks out. However, I want to double-check: in the G-GLM setting, the Hessian (4) uses D = diag{ℓ''(y_i, w^T x_i)}. With square loss, ℓ''=1 identically, so D = I_n and H = XX^T/n, which gives the Marčenko–Pastur law (not rescaled, but with c = p/n). This is consistent with the claim. No issue.

comments.title: Phase transition threshold stated as √c rather than c
comments.quote: the largest Hessian eigenvalue is bound to become asymptotically isolated once _∥_ _**µ** ∥_[2] exceeds _[√] c_
comments.explanation: In the classical spiked covariance/BBP phase transition for sample covariance matrices with ratio c = p/n, the threshold for an isolated spike is typically at signal strength √c (for the eigenvalue of X X^T / n scaled appropriately). The text says the spike appears once ‖μ‖² exceeds √c. Given ρ = lim ‖μ‖² is defined as the signal strength, the threshold on ρ being √c is plausible and consistent with classical BBP-type results for the rescaled MP law (here rescaled by g = 1/4 per Corollary 1). So the statement ‖μ‖² > √c corresponds to ρ > √c, which is the standard form. This is likely correct; I initially worried whether the threshold should be c rather than √c, but for the BBP transition on the covariance (1/n) X X^T with spike μμ^T, the threshold on ‖μ‖² is indeed √c. No issue.

comments.title: Corollary 2 fixed-point equation appears to miss a term
comments.quote: the Stieltjes transform m ( z ) satisfies m ( z ) = 1 / (E[ f ( r, z )] − z ) for f ( r, z ) = 1 / ( cm ( z ) + 2 + e^{−r} + e^r )
comments.explanation: I want to sanity-check the form of f(r,z). For logistic loss, g = ℓ''(y,h) = e^{-yh}/(1+e^{-yh})² = 1/(2 + e^{-yh} + e^{yh}). With μ=0 and w*=0 (so y is symmetric Bernoulli independent of x under the logistic model with w*=0), h = w^T x ~ N(0, ‖w‖²), and effectively g ~ 1/(2 + e^{-r} + e^r) for r ~ N(0, ‖w‖²). The separable-covariance fixed-point equation typically takes the form m(z) = ∫ ν(dg) / (g/(1 + c·g·m̃(z)) − z) or similar. The given form m(z) = 1/(E[f(r,z)] − z) with f(r,z) = 1/(cm(z) + 2 + e^{-r} + e^r) doesn't have the usual g factor in the numerator; instead the denominator has cm(z) added to (2 + e^{-r} + e^r) = 1/g. This is consistent with the identity g/(1+c·g·m(z)) = 1/(1/g + c·m(z)) = 1/(2 + e^{-r} + e^r + c·m(z)). So the formula is internally consistent. Fine.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and timely problem—understanding Hessian spectra beyond overly idealized random-matrix assumptions—and the framing is strong: the motivation is clear, the gap between classical RMT models and observed ML Hessians is well articulated, and the proposed G-GLM setting appears well chosen as a tractable but nontrivial bridge between theory and practice. The abstract and introduction convey significant potential contributions, especially the promise of precise spectral characterizations exhibiting multi-bulk structure, unbounded support, and isolated eigenvalues depending on data, model, and loss. However, the beginning also raises some clarity and positioning issues: the technical setup is introduced somewhat abruptly, key definitions and assumptions are not yet fully explicit, and the relationship between the proposed G-GLM class, classical GLMs, and neural-network-relevant models could be explained more cleanly for readers outside random matrix theory. The manuscript would benefit from an earlier, more concrete summary of the main theorems, assumptions, and practical implications, as well as sharper differentiation from prior spiked-model and Hessian-spectrum analyses. There are also presentation concerns—some notation is dense, several equations are essential but not easy to parse in the provided text, and the introduction occasionally overstates connections to “realistic” ML models before demonstrating how far the simplified model actually transfers. Overall, the work appears technically ambitious and potentially high impact, but its effectiveness will depend on whether the later sections deliver transparent assumptions, interpretable spectral formulas, convincing empirical validation, and a careful discussion of the limits of extrapolating from G-GLMs to modern neural networks.

comments.title: Second-order eigenvalue signs are described as a definitive test for extrema
comments.quote: the signs of the smallest and largest Hessian eigenvalue can be used to test whether a given **w** is a local maximum, local minimum, or a saddle point.
comments.explanation: The Hessian spectrum gives the advertised curvature information in the nondegenerate case: positive definiteness, negative definiteness, and indefiniteness imply a strict local minimum, strict local maximum, and saddle-type behavior respectively. But the statement is too categorical if zero eigenvalues are possible. For semidefinite or degenerate Hessians, the signs of the smallest and largest eigenvalues are inconclusive and higher-order terms may determine the local behavior. The passage should qualify the claim as a nondegenerate second-order test.

comments.title: Phase-retrieval informative eigenvector appears to use an ambiguous or possibly wrong Hessian side/sign
comments.quote: the top eigenvector (that corresponds to the largest eigenvalue) contains structural information of the underlying model, in the sense that it is positively correlated with **w** _∗_ ...; ... the associated dominant eigenvector can still be used to recover **w** almost perfectly
comments.explanation: For the stated phase-retrieval square loss, \(\ell(y,h)=(y-h^2)^2/4\), the curvature is \(\ell''(y,h)=3h^2-y\), so \(H=n^{-1}\sum_i(3(w^Tx_i)^2-y_i)x_ix_i^T\). At \(w=0\), the informative matrix is essentially \(-H=n^{-1}\sum_i y_i x_ix_i^T\), so the planted vector aligns with a most-negative Hessian direction rather than necessarily with the largest algebraic eigenvalue of \(H\). Later, the paper also discusses a model-induced spike on the left side of the bulk, where calling the corresponding eigenvector “top” or “dominant” is ambiguous. The text should specify whether it means largest algebraic eigenvalue, largest magnitude, smallest eigenvalue, or an eigenvector of a sign-flipped/preprocessed matrix.

comments.title: Figure 1b is not reproducible because the independent choice of \(\mathbf w\) is underspecified
comments.quote: we compare, in Fig 1a and 1b, the Hessian eigenvalues for the logistic model (2) with the logistic loss ... for different choices of **w** in the parameter space.
comments.explanation: The comparison is meant to show that the Hessian spectrum depends on the evaluation point \(\mathbf w\), but the “indep. \(\mathbf w\)” case in Fig. 1b is not fully specified. The distribution, norm, and overlaps of \(\mathbf w\) with \(\mathbf w_*\) and \(\boldsymbol\mu\) matter for the limiting spectrum and possible spikes. Without these details, the illustrative experiment is not reproducible.

comments.title: Curvature-weight distributions in unbounded-support examples are overstated
comments.quote: with say **w** _∗_ = **w** , _g_ follows a chi-square distribution with one degree of freedom ...; ... for the logistic model (2) with \(\ell(y,h)=\exp(-yh)\), we have that \(g=\exp(-yh)\) ... which follows a log-normal distribution
comments.explanation: The intended conclusion that these curvature weights can have unbounded support is correct, but the distributional descriptions are too specific. In phase retrieval, if \(\mathbf w_* = \mathbf w\), then \(g=2(\mathbf w^T\mathbf x)^2\), which is generally a scaled noncentral \(\chi^2_1\), not simply \(\chi^2_1\). For exponential loss under a logistic response model, \(g=e^{-yh}\) is not generally log-normal because \(y\) is random and dependent on \(\mathbf x\); it becomes log-normal only in special centered/symmetric cases. These examples should be stated as unbounded-support cases without the overly narrow distributional claims.

comments.title: PSD covariance-type spectral conditions are used without enough qualification for indefinite Hessians
comments.quote: Since the Hessian eigenvalue distribution µ is of compact support if and only if both ν and ν̃ have compact support ...; Uniqueness is ensured ... and _zm(z)<0_ ...
comments.explanation: Several statements appear to import conditions standard for nonnegative sample-covariance-type matrices into a setting where \(g=\ell''(y,h)\) may be negative and the Hessian may be indefinite. The real-axis condition \(z m(z)<0\) is not a general Stieltjes-transform characterization for measures supported on all of \(\mathbb R\). Similarly, the compact-support “if and only if” result for separable covariance models usually needs positivity and nondegeneracy assumptions; with indefinite or degenerate factors, the statement is not automatically valid as written. The paper should either restrict these claims to the positive/nondegenerate setting or state the additional hypotheses under which they apply.

comments.title: Preprocessing bounded curvature weights changes the matrix, so untrimmed examples are not directly covered
comments.quote: after the application of some function \(f\) so that \(f(g)\) is bounded ...; This is the case in Fig 1d, where the top eigenvector is observed to be a “noisy” version of the model parameter \(\mathbf w_*\).
comments.explanation: Applying a bounded preprocessing function to \(g=\ell''(y,h)\) generally produces a different Hessian-type spectral matrix, not the Hessian of the original empirical risk unless it is explicitly tied to a modified loss. This matters because the phase-retrieval square-loss example has unbounded curvature weights, while the isolated-eigenvalue theorems require bounded support or preprocessing. Citing the untrimmed Fig. 1d as “the case” motivating the theorem may therefore mislead readers into thinking the theorem applies directly to the original untrimmed Hessian. The authors should distinguish the original Hessian from the preprocessed/trimmed matrix and clarify whether Fig. 1d is only a finite-sample heuristic.

comments.title: Outlier and eigenvector claims need explicit outside-bulk admissibility conditions
comments.quote: for \(\lambda\) such that \(\mathbf G(\lambda)\) has a zero eigenvalue ...; \(\lambda_w\) solution to \(0=\det \mathbf G(\lambda_w)\) ...; the associated isolated eigenvector ... as \(\|\mathbf w\|\) grows large
comments.explanation: The determinant equation is the standard mechanism for locating outliers, but it should be interpreted only for admissible real roots outside the limiting spectral support, typically separated from the bulk and satisfying simplicity/nondegeneracy conditions. Roots inside the bulk or at a bulk edge do not by themselves predict isolated eigenvalues. The same qualification is needed for the later eigenvector-alignment discussion: once the spike has merged into the limiting bulk, there is no longer an asymptotically isolated eigenvalue/eigenvector pair to which outlier-overlap intuition directly applies. The theorem and corollary should state the outside-support condition explicitly, and the large-\(\|w\|\) alignment observation should be framed as finite-sample or restricted to the outlier regime.

comments.title: Overstatement that any deviation from the logistic/identity-covariance setting yields a very non-MP spectrum
comments.quote: any change in the response model ... in the choice of loss function ... or beyond the identity covariance setting ... would induce a Hessian spectrum that is very different from the Marcenko-Pastur law.
comments.explanation: The substantive point is correct that the Hessian spectrum is not universally Marchenko–Pastur and can depend strongly on the curvature distribution and covariance spectrum. But the categorical wording “any change” and “would induce” is too strong. Some changes preserve an MP or scaled-MP limiting law, finite-rank covariance perturbations may change only spikes, and small perturbations need not produce a visually very different spectrum. The claim should be softened to say that such changes can induce substantial departures, as demonstrated by the examples.

comments.title: Hessian formula omits the explicit second derivative
comments.explanation: The surrounding sentence correctly says that the Hessian is the second derivative of the loss with respect to the parameter vector, so the intended meaning is clear. However, the displayed formula writes only \(\partial L(\mathbf w)/(\partial \mathbf w\,\partial \mathbf w^T)\), rather than the standard \(\partial^2 L(\mathbf w)/(\partial \mathbf w\,\partial \mathbf w^T)\) or \(\nabla^2 L(\mathbf w)\). I considered whether this could be an OCR omission, and it likely is; nevertheless, as written it is mathematically imprecise in a definition appearing at the start of the paper. The issue is localized and easily fixed.

comments.title: Second-order eigenvalue signs are described as a definitive test for extrema
comments.quote: the signs of the smallest and largest Hessian eigenvalue can be used to test whether a given **w** is a local maximum, local minimum, or a saddle point.
comments.explanation: The authors are making a standard introductory point: the Hessian spectrum gives local curvature information, and in the nondegenerate case positive definiteness, negative definiteness, or indefiniteness is highly informative. However, the wording is stronger than the usual second-order conditions support. For a twice differentiable unconstrained objective, a positive definite Hessian is sufficient for a strict local minimum, a negative definite Hessian is sufficient for a strict local maximum, and an indefinite Hessian rules out a local extremum and indicates a saddle-type point. But if the Hessian is only semidefinite or has zero eigenvalues, the signs of the smallest and largest eigenvalues are inconclusive; higher-order terms can determine the local behavior. Thus this is acceptable as informal motivation, but as phrased it could mislead a careful reader into thinking Hessian eigenvalue signs always provide a complete test.

comments.title: Phase-retrieval initialization claim appears to use the wrong Hessian eigenvector/sign
comments.quote: For phase retrieval model _y_ = ( **w** _∗_[T] **[x]**[)][2][with square loss] _[ ℓ]_[(] _[y, h]_[) = (] _[y][ −][h]_[2][)][2] _[/]_[4][, the] non-convex nature of the problem is reflected by a (relatively large) fraction of negative Hessian eigenvalues in Fig 1c. We also note that the top eigenvector (that corresponds to the largest eigenvalue) contains structural information of the underlying model, in the sense that it is positively correlated with **w** _∗_ , as predicted by our theory.
comments.explanation: The qualitative point that the phase-retrieval Hessian can contain information about the planted vector is plausible, and it is consistent with the broader spectral-initialization literature. However, after checking the actual Hessian for the stated loss, the specific claim about the “top eigenvector” of the Hessian is not clearly correct. For \(\ell(y,h)=(y-h^2)^2/4\), one has \(\ell''(y,h)=3h^2-y\), so \(H=n^{-1}\sum_i(3(w^Tx_i)^2-y_i)x_ix_i^T\). At \(w=0\), the informative phase-retrieval matrix is essentially \(-H=n^{-1}\sum_i y_i x_ix_i^T\), so \(w_*\) aligns with the most negative Hessian direction, not the largest algebraic eigenvalue. At a nonzero independent \(w\), the population Hessian also contains a positive spike in the \(w\) direction, while the \(w_*\) contribution enters with the opposite sign. Thus a reader could reasonably expect the planted vector to be associated with a left-hand outlier or with the top eigenvector of a sign-flipped/preprocessed matrix, not necessarily with the largest eigenvalue of \(H\) itself. This concern is reinforced by the later discussion that, without preprocessing/trimming, the apparent isolated eigenvalue in the phase-retrieval example may be a finite-dimensional effect that is buried asymptotically. The text should specify whether “top” means largest algebraic eigenvalue, largest magnitude eigenvalue, smallest eigenvalue, or the top eigenvector after negating/preprocessing the Hessian, and under what conditions the theory predicts correlation with \(w_*\).

comments.title: Figure 1b is not reproducible because the independent choice of \(\mathbf w\) is underspecified
comments.quote: To have a more clear picture of our contribution, we compare, in Fig 1a and 1b, the Hessian eigenvalues for the logistic model (2) with the logistic loss _ℓ_ ( _y, h_ ) = ln(1 + _e[−][yh]_ ) ... for different choices of **w** in the parameter space.
comments.explanation: The comparison between Fig. 1a and Fig. 1b is meant to illustrate a real and important point: the Hessian spectrum depends on the evaluation point \(\mathbf w\), not only on the response model and feature covariance. The setup gives \(p\), \(n\), \(C\), and apparently \(\mathbf w_* = \boldsymbol\mu\), but for the “indep. \(\mathbf w\)” case in Fig. 1b the distribution, norm, and correlations of \(\mathbf w\) with \(\mathbf w_*\) and \(\boldsymbol\mu\) are not stated in the passage/caption. This matters technically because the limiting law and possible spike depend on quantities such as \(\mathbf w^T C\mathbf w\), \(\mathbf w^T\boldsymbol\mu\), and overlaps with \(\mathbf w_*\). If \(\mathbf w\) is drawn, for example, from \(N(0,I_p/p)\), fixed orthogonal to \(\mathbf w_*\), normalized in some other way, or chosen deterministically, the observed spike behavior can change. This is a localized reproducibility issue for the illustrative experiment rather than a problem with the general theory.

comments.title: Phase retrieval curvature is a scaled/noncentral chi-square, not generally χ² with one degree of freedom
comments.quote: with say **w** _∗_ = **w** , _g_ follows a chi-square distribution with one degree of freedom and has thus _unbounded_ support.
comments.explanation: The preceding computation of the curvature for the phase retrieval square loss is right: for \(\ell(y,h)=(y-h^2)^2/4\), one gets \(\ell''(y,h)=3h^2-y\), hence \(g=3(\mathbf w^\top\mathbf x)^2-(\mathbf w_*^\top\mathbf x)^2\). The intended conclusion—that the law of \(g\) can have unbounded support—is also correct. However, if \(\mathbf w_* = \mathbf w\), then \(g=2(\mathbf w^\top\mathbf x)^2\), not simply a chi-square random variable with one degree of freedom. Under the stated Gaussian feature model, \(\mathbf w^\top\mathbf x\sim N(\mathbf w^\top\boldsymbol\mu,\mathbf w^\top\mathbf C\mathbf w)\), so \(g\) is in general a scaled noncentral \(\chi^2_1\); only under additional normalization and zero-mean assumptions would it reduce, up to a factor, to a central chi-square. This does not affect the unbounded-support point, but the distributional statement as written is inaccurate.

comments.title: Stieltjes-transform uniqueness condition appears tailored to nonnegative spectra despite later indefinite Hessians
comments.quote: Uniqueness is ensured in such a way that _ℑ_ [ _m_ ( _z_ )] _· ℑ_ [ _z_ ] _>_ 0 for _ℑ_ [ _z_ ] _̸_ = 0 and _zm_ ( _z_ ) _<_ 0 for _ℑ_ [ _z_ ] = 0, so that ( _z, m_ ( _z_ )) is a valid Stieltjes transform couple, see more details in [29].
comments.explanation: The sign condition on the imaginary parts is the standard Stieltjes-transform condition, and for positive semidefinite sample-covariance-type matrices one often also uses sign restrictions such as \(z m(z)<0\) on the real axis outside the positive support. What gave me pause is that this paper explicitly treats non-convex losses and gives phase retrieval as an example with negative Hessian eigenvalues. For probability measures supported on all of \(\mathbb R\), there is no general real-axis condition \(z m(z)<0\): for example, for a measure supported at a negative point, this inequality can fail for real negative \(z\) outside the support. The usual general characterization would instead combine the upper/lower half-plane mapping property with the normalization \(m(z)\sim -1/z\) at infinity, plus the relevant domain conditions. If the fixed-point equation in Theorem 1 is intended to cover indefinite Hessians, this uniqueness statement needs qualification; otherwise it reads as if the limiting measure were supported on \(\mathbb R_+\), which conflicts with the non-convex examples discussed immediately afterward.

comments.title: Compact-support equivalence may require extra nondegeneracy/positivity qualifications
comments.quote: Since the Hessian eigenvalue distribution µ is of compact support_ if and only if _both ν and ν_ ˜ _have compact support [17, Porposition 3.4], µ may be of unbounded support, depending on the model and the loss._
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: in separable covariance/free multiplicative-convolution settings, boundedness of the limiting spectrum is closely tied to boundedness of the distributions of the covariance and diagonal factors, and this is a useful way to explain why exponential loss or phase retrieval can produce heavy/unbounded spectral support. However, the stated “if and only if” seems stronger than what is justified under the assumptions as written. First, the cited separable-covariance compact-support result is usually formulated for nonnegative covariance-type factors, whereas here \(g=\ell''(y,h)\) may be negative, so the Hessian can be indefinite. Second, Assumption 1 bounds \(\|\mathbf C\|\) but does not impose a uniform lower bound or a nontrivial limiting mass away from zero; in degenerate limiting cases, an unbounded diagonal factor need not automatically imply an unbounded limiting product spectrum. The examples that follow, such as \(\mathbf C=\mathbf I\), are fine, and the conclusion is correct there. The general equivalence would be safer with the standard positivity/nondegeneracy conditions made explicit, or phrased as a sufficient explanation for the examples rather than an unconditional iff in the present generality.

comments.title: Exponential-loss curvature is not generally log-normal under the logistic response model
comments.quote: Precisely, consider the logistic model (2) with \(\ell(y,h) = \exp(-yh)\), we have that \(g = \exp(-yh)\) for \(h \sim \mathcal N(\mathbf w^T\boldsymbol\mu, \mathbf w^T\mathbf C\mathbf w)\) which follows a log-normal distribution and has unbounded support.
comments.explanation: The curvature calculation itself is right for the usual binary logistic convention \(y\in\{\pm1\}\): \(\partial_h^2 e^{-yh}=y^2e^{-yh}=e^{-yh}\). The unbounded-support conclusion is also basically right. What is not generally right is the distributional statement that \(g\) is log-normal. Since \(y\) is a random response generated from the logistic model and is generally dependent on \(\mathbf x\), the product \(yh\), with \(h=\mathbf w^T\mathbf x\), is not generally Gaussian. Even if \(y\) were independent Rademacher, \(yh\) would be a mixture of \(N(m,s^2)\) and \(N(-m,s^2)\), not Gaussian unless the mean is zero. In the specific numerical setting of Fig. 2, where \(\mathbf w_*=0\) and \(\boldsymbol\mu=0\), the statement may become true because \(y\) is independent and symmetric and \(h\) is centered. But as written it is presented for the logistic model generally. A safer statement would be that \(g\) is unbounded, and in the centered null-label special case it is log-normal.

comments.title: Applying a bounded preprocessing function changes the matrix being studied
comments.quote: Under this condition (or, after the application of some function \(f\) so that \(f(g)\) is bounded), we can then talk about the (possible) isolated Hessian eigenvalues, as in the following result
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: this connects to the trimming/preprocessing strategy used in phase retrieval and related spectral initialization methods, where heavy-tailed weights are truncated to make outlier analysis meaningful. The concern is that replacing \(g=\ell''(y,h)\) by \(f(g)\) no longer gives the Hessian of the original empirical risk unless \(f(g)\) happens to be the second derivative of some modified loss evaluated at the same data. It gives a preprocessed Hessian-type or spectral matrix. Since the sentence continues to refer to “Hessian eigenvalues,” a reader could mistakenly think Theorems 2–3 apply directly to the original Hessian after merely post-processing the analysis, rather than to a different trimmed matrix. This is fixable by saying “isolated eigenvalues of the corresponding preprocessed Hessian-type matrix” or by explicitly defining the modified loss/matrix.

comments.title: Outlier equation appears to be stated without explicitly restricting \(\lambda\) outside the limiting bulk
comments.quote: Then, for \(\lambda\) such that \(\mathbf G(\lambda)\) has a zero eigenvalue (of multiplicity one), there exists an eigenvalue \(\hat\lambda\) of \(\mathbf H\) such that \(\hat\lambda - \lambda \xrightarrow{a.s.} 0\).
comments.explanation: The surrounding prose makes clear that Theorem 2 is intended to characterize isolated eigenvalues, and in standard spiked/random-matrix outlier analyses the determinant equation is interpreted on the real line outside the limiting spectral support, usually with additional separation from the bulk edge. If that restriction is included in the omitted displayed definition of \(\mathbf G\), then the issue is only expository. But in the visible theorem statement, the condition is phrased simply as “\(\lambda\) such that \(\mathbf G(\lambda)\) has a zero eigenvalue,” without saying \(\lambda\notin\operatorname{supp}\mu\) or lies in a spectral gap. Roots of an analytic continuation inside the bulk would not correspond to isolated eigenvalues in the usual sense; moreover, eigenvalues of \(\mathbf H\) are dense in the bulk regardless of \(\det \mathbf G\), so the statement would lose its intended outlier meaning. This should be made explicit at the theorem statement level.

comments.title: Fig. 1d is used as an illustration in a section whose assumptions exclude the untrimmed phase-retrieval example
comments.quote: This is the case in Fig 1d, where the top eigenvector is observed to be a “noisy” version of the model parameter \(\mathbf w_*\).
comments.explanation: The qualitative observation in Fig. 1d is plausible and aligns with the intuition from spectral methods for phase retrieval. However, the immediately preceding setup for Theorems 2–3 requires bounded support of the law of \(g\), or a preprocessing \(f\) that makes the weights bounded. Earlier, the paper explicitly notes that for the square-loss phase retrieval Hessian, \(g=3(\mathbf w^T\mathbf x)^2-(\mathbf w_*^T\mathbf x)^2\) has unbounded support and that apparent isolated eigenvalues may be finite-dimensional effects unless trimming is applied. Fig. 1d appears to be the untrimmed square-loss phase-retrieval example. Thus, citing it here as “the case” motivating the isolated-eigenvector theorem may mislead readers into thinking Theorem 3 applies directly to that displayed experiment. The authors could clarify that Fig. 1d is a finite-sample heuristic illustration, not an example covered by the bounded-support theorem unless an appropriate trimming/preprocessing is used.

comments.title: Overstatement that any deviation from the logistic/identity-covariance setting yields a very non-MP spectrum
comments.quote: Nonetheless, it must be pointed out that this “visual approximation” by Marcenko-Pastur law is˘ _not robust_ , in the sense that it “visually” holds only for, yet formally different from, the case of (i) logistic model with (ii) logistic loss and (iii) identity covariance **C** = **I** _p_ : any change in the response model (e.g., the phase retrieval model in Fig 1c), in the choice of loss function (e.g., the exponential loss in Fig 2b), or beyond the identity covariance setting (as in Fig 2c and 2d) would induce a Hessian spectrum that is very different from the Marcenko-Pastur law.˘
comments.explanation:
I understand the authors’ intent here: Theorem 1 shows that the limiting spectrum depends on the curvature distribution \(
u\) and the covariance spectrum \(\widetilde
u\), so the Marčenko–Pastur approximation should not be treated as universal. The examples cited—phase retrieval, exponential loss, and heterogeneous covariance—do indeed demonstrate large departures from an MP-like shape. However, the wording “any change” and “would induce” is stronger than what the theorem supports. Some changes preserve an MP or scaled-MP law: for example, with constant \(g\) and \(\mathbf C=a\mathbf I_p\), the spectrum is just a rescaled MP law; finite-rank covariance perturbations can leave the limiting empirical spectral distribution MP-like while producing only spikes; and changing the response model may not affect the Hessian bulk when the loss curvature is constant. Even small non-identity perturbations of \(\widetilde
u\) need not make the spectrum “very different” in a visual or asymptotic-continuity sense. The substantive point is correct if phrased as “can induce” or “the examples show that such changes may induce,” but the current categorical statement overstates the consequence of Theorem 1.

comments.title: Outlier equation is stated without the necessary outside-support condition
comments.quote: Moreover, there is_ at most one _isolated eigenvalue λ_[ˆ] **w** _of_ **H** _that is due to_ **w** _and satisfies λ_ ˆ **w** _− λ_ **w** _−−→a.s._ 0 _with λ_ **w** _solution to_ 0 = det **G** ( _λ_ **w** ) = 1 + _m_ ( _λ_ **w** )[E][[] _[f]_[(] _[r][,][λ]_ **[w]** _∥_[)(] **w** _[r] ∥_[2][2] _[−][∥]_ **[w]** _[∥]_[2][)]] _.
comments.explanation: I read this as the specialized outlier equation obtained from the finite-dimensional determinant condition in the earlier isolated-eigenvalue theorem. That is the right general mechanism: isolated eigenvalues are typically located by zeros of a small deterministic matrix such as G(z). However, in standard spiked random matrix results, the determinant equation is only an outlier-location equation for real roots lying outside the limiting spectral support, usually with a nonzero distance from the bulk edge and appropriate simplicity/nondegeneracy conditions. The surrounding prose later says the spike can be absent or can merge into the main bulk, so the authors clearly understand this issue. What remains problematic is that the formal corollary as quoted does not state the outside-support/root-admissibility condition. A reader trying to use the displayed equation directly could mistake roots of det G inside the bulk, or at a bulk edge, as predicting isolated eigenvalues. This is localized and fixable by adding an explicit condition such as λ_w ∉ supp(μ), with positive limiting separation from the support.

comments.title: Alignment is discussed after the outlier has merged into the bulk
comments.quote: It is perhaps even more surprising to observe in Fig 5-(right) that, the alignment between the associated isolated eigenvector and the parameter **w** is, unlike the eigengap in Fig 5-(middle), monotonically increasing as _∥_ **w** _∥_ grows large, as in the case of Fig 4-(right). This suggests that, in the case of spike due to model, a _smaller_ eigengap may not always imply _less_ statistical “information” contained in the associated eigenvector, which somehow goes against the conventional eigengap heuristic [67, 35].
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is interesting and plausible in finite samples: the informative direction can remain visible even when the absolute eigengap becomes small. I also checked that the authors immediately before this have described a non-monotone eigengap and a spike that eventually “merges into” the main bulk, so they are not simply claiming a persistent BBP-type outlier. The remaining concern is terminological and asymptotic: once the spike has merged into the limiting bulk, there is no longer an asymptotically isolated eigenvalue/eigenvector pair in the usual spiked-model sense. Eigenvectors associated with bulk or edge eigenvalues are generally not characterized by the same outlier-overlap formulas and can be unstable unless an additional selection rule or scaling regime is specified. Thus the phrase “the associated isolated eigenvector” is potentially misleading for the large-∥w∥ regime where the eigengap has fallen to zero. This should be qualified as a finite-n empirical observation, or restricted to the parameter range where the solution remains outside the limiting support.

comments.title: “Dominant eigenvector” is ambiguous for a left-hand-side spike
comments.quote: empirical results suggest that for extremely large _∥_ **w** _∥_ , the eigengap tends to “vanish”, the associated dominant eigenvector can still be used to recover **w** almost perfectly, see [43, Fig 8] as an example.
comments.explanation: The authors have just emphasized that, in the setting of Fig. 5, the model-induced spike appears on the left-hand side of the bulk, i.e. it is a small eigenvalue rather than the largest Hessian eigenvalue. In much of numerical linear algebra and spectral-initialization literature, “dominant eigenvector” usually means the eigenvector associated with the largest algebraic eigenvalue, or sometimes the largest-magnitude eigenvalue. Here the informative vector appears to be associated with the left outlier/smallest eigenvalue, or perhaps with an extreme eigenvector selected by a problem-specific rule. The intended meaning is probably clear from the surrounding discussion, but the wording could mislead a reader into looking at the top eigenvector rather than the left-edge eigenvector. Replacing “dominant” with “corresponding extreme/left-outlier eigenvector” or explicitly saying “smallest-eigenvalue eigenvector” would remove the ambiguity.
